comment.body:
Hi Alma, coming back to your comment in Friday’s chat: “Then boom, vol squeeze when EU responds; especially if they sell debt – as I expect.”

You’ve mentioned that the EU tariffs come at a time when the EU economy is still grappling with post-pandemic recovery and external shocks, and that these tariffs are being used to pressure the EU into accepting the "Holy Alliance 2.0" peace proposal. With Trump aiming to solve Western stagflation by Easter - and deliberately engineering a mild recession tocreate a negative wealth effect, while carefully avoiding a deflationary spiral - we’ve already seen a drop in the US 10Y yield and significantly falling oil prices. That suggests deflation fears are beginning to creep into investor sentiment, and that everything is currently going according to plan.

I’m trying to understand your thinking around why you expect the EU to strike back by selling U.S. debt. As you pointed out, such a move could depress bond prices, push up interest rates, and potentially destabilize global markets. Do you think they would go this far precisely to disrupt Trump’s strategy (e.g., inducing lower rates, etc.) or do you see this as part of the overall plan?

Specifically the cost of technology must be significantly lower so that arab investors find it an attractive deal (mission almost complete?). After buying tech at lower costs, funds can be allocated into debt instruments and bonds (where a selloff in bonds triggered by the EU creates strong entry points?)
comment.raw_body:

You’ve mentioned that the EU tariffs come at a time when the EU economy is still grappling with post-pandemic recovery and external shocks, and that these tariffs are being used to pressure the EU into accepting the "Holy Alliance 2.0" peace proposal. With Trump aiming to solve Western stagflation by Easter - and deliberately engineering a mild recession tocreate a negative wealth effect, while carefully avoiding a deflationary spiral - we’ve already seen a drop in the US 10Y yield and significantly falling oil prices. That suggests deflation fears are beginning to creep into investor sentiment, and that everything is currently going according to plan.

I’m trying to understand your thinking around why you expect the EU to strike back by selling U.S. debt. As you pointed out, such a move could depress bond prices, push up interest rates, and potentially destabilize global markets. Do you think they would go this far precisely to disrupt Trump’s strategy (e.g., inducing lower rates, etc.) or do you see this as part of the overall plan?

Specifically the cost of technology must be significantly lower so that arab investors find it an attractive deal (mission almost complete?). After buying tech at lower costs, funds can be allocated into debt instruments and bonds (where a selloff in bonds triggered by the EU creates strong entry points?)

comment.body:
I keep telling you...
a lot of charlatan out there.
If you want to know the greeks check Natenberg, Bennett and Taleb's books (Taleb is the most mathematical one, and Bennett is the most practical one)
And on the fintwit world, it is only Volsignals who actually knows these concepts, and some part of the inner structure of the markets. He is rather a commentator than an analyst as much as I know, but he won't tell me bullshit about the greeks for sure. 

But greeks are not everything. Somebody said Lyn Alden. She is a very good macro analyst/commentator. 
I'm pretty sure there are others as well, but to be honest I'm not an active twitter user in this field... my following list is mostly photographers, and my feed is full of artistic photos and ballet pics
